instructor.name: Dr. Maryam Zahabi
instructor.office_hours: 2:00 PM – 4:00 PM, Mondays or by appointment

chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number: ISEN 634
Course Title: Human Performance Modeling
Section: 601
Time: MW 04:10 PM-05:25 PM
Location: ZACH 361
Credit Hours: 3

Instructor Details
Instructor: Dr. Maryam Zahabi
Office: ETB 4044
Phone: 979-458-2354
E-Mail: mzahabi@tamu.edu
Office Hours: 2:00 PM – 4:00 PM, Mondays or by appointment

Course Description
Examination of models of the human as an information processor and exploration of advanced theory on human cognition; situational awareness and workload responses of humans in complex tasks, description of cognitive task performance and modeling of user behavior in specific applications using task analysis and cognitive performance modeling methods, e.g., goals, operators, methods, and selection rules (GOMS), keystroke-level model (KLM), critical path method GOMS (CPM-GOMS); introduction of advanced computational approaches to human behavior modeling including executive process-interactive control (EPIC), adaptive control of thought-rational (ACT-R), and queueing network-model human processor (QN-MHP); development of computational cognitive models of human performance using different software tools (e.g., CogTool, Cogulator) to evaluate an existing interface.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Graduate classification; ISEN 330 or ISEN 630 or approval of instructor.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Below is a list of readings required for in-class discussions (the list might change during the semester).
[1] Information Processing Theory:
Cowan, N. (1988). Evolving conceptions of memory storage, selective attention, and their mutual constraints within the human information-processing system. Psychological Bulletin, 104(2), 163-191.
Flach, J. M. (1999). Beyond Error: The language of coordination and stability. In Hancock, P (Ed.). Human Performance and Ergonomics. (pp. 109-128). Academic Press.
Nickerson, R. S. (1999). Engineering psychology and ergonomics. In E. C. Carterette & M. Friedman (Eds.), Human performance and ergonomics: Vol. 17. Handbook of perception and cognition (pp. 1-45). San Diego, CA: Academic Press.
Wicken, C. D. (1992). Introduction to engineering psychology and human performance, Engineering psychology and human performance, 1-23.
[2] Situation Awareness:
Endsley, M. R. (1995a). Toward a theory of situation awareness in dynamic systems. Human Factors, 37(1),32-64.
Endsley, M. R. (1995b). Measurement of situation awareness in dynamic systems. Human Factors, 37(1), 65-84.
Smith, K. & Hancock, PA (1995). Situation awareness is adaptive, externally directed. consciousness. Human Factors, 37(1), 137-148.
Endsley, M. R. (2016). Designing for situation awareness: An approach to user-centered design. CRC press.
[3] Mental Workload:
Hart, S. G. & Staveland, L. E. (1988). Development of NASA-TLX (Task Load Index): Results of empirical and theoretical research. In P. A. Hancock and N. Meshkati (Eds.), Human Mental Workload (pp. 139-183). North-Holland Elsevier Science: Amsterdam.
Pauzié, A. (2008). A method to assess the driver mental workload: The driving activity load index (DALI). IET Intelligent Transport Systems, 2(4), 315-322.
[4] Task Analysis:
Gordon, S. E. & Gill, R. T. (1997). Cognitive task analysis. In C.E. Zsambok & G. Klein (Eds.) Naturalistic Decision Making. (131-141).
Kieras, D. E. (1997a). Task analysis and the design of functionality. In A. Tucker (Ed.), The Computer Science and Engineering Handbook (pp. 1401-1423). Boca Raton, FL: CRC Inc.
[5] User Modeling:
Card, S. K., Moran, T. P. & Newell, A. (1983). The Psychology of Human-Computer Interaction. Hillsdale, NJ: Lawrence Erlbaum.
John, B. E. and Kieras, D. E. (1996b). The GOMS family of user interface analysis techniques: comparison and contrast. ACM Transactions on Computer-Human Interaction, 3(4): 320-351.
Kieras, D. (1994). A guide to GOMS task analysis. University of Michigan.
Kieras, D. (2001). Using the keystroke-level model to estimate execution times. University of Michigan, 555.
[6] Advanced Computational Cognitive Modeling Techniques:
Kieras, D. & Meyer, D.E. (1997). An overview of the EPIC architecture for cognition and performance with application to human-computer interaction. Human-Computer Interaction, 12, 391-438.
John, B., Vera, A., Matessa, M., Freed, M., & Remington, R. (2002, April). Automating cpm-goms. In Proceedings of the SIGCHI conference on Human factors in computing systems (pp. 147-154). ACM.
Liu, Y., Feyen, R., & Tsimhoni, O. (2006). Queueing Network-Model Human Processor (QN-MHP): A computational architecture for multitask performance in human-machine systems. ACM Transactions on Computer-Human Interaction (TOCHI), 13(1), 37-70.
Yuan, H., Li, S., Rusconi, P., & Aljaffan, N. (2017, July). When eye-tracking meets cognitive modeling: applications to cyber security systems. In International Conference on Human Aspects of Information Security, Privacy, and Trust (pp. 251-264). Springer, Cham.
Zahabi, M., White, M. M., Zhang, W., Winslow, A. T., Zhang, F., Huang, H., & Kaber, D. В. (2019). Application of Cognitive Task Performance Modeling for Assessing Usability of Transradial Prostheses. IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems.
Jeon, M., Nadri, C., & Zhang, Y. (2021). Introduction of a computational modelling approach to auditory display research: Case studies using the QN-MHP framework. Proceedings of the 26th International Conference on Auditory Display (ICAD2021), Virtual Conference, June 25-28.
Park, J., Wozniak, D., & Zahabi, M. (2024). Modeling novice law enforcement officers' interaction with in-vehicle technology. Applied Ergonomics, 114, 104154.
Park, J., & Zahabi, M. (2024). A review of human performance models for prediction of driver behavior and interactions with in-vehicle technology. Human factors, 66(4), 1249-1275.
Tan, X., & Zhang, Y. (2025). Understanding driver response to multi-stage takeover requests across varied modalities: A computational cognitive modeling approach. Transportation Research Part C: Emerging Technologies, 174, 105114.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Adding points earned on each submission and dividing this number by the total number of points possible will determine final grades. Below is a summary of the course assignments and their point values.
| Submission | Total Points | Approx. Percent of Grade |
|---|---|---|
| Exam | 150 | 25% |
| Homework (3@50 pts. each) | 150 | 25% |
| Article Presentation | 100 | 17% |
| Term Project | 200 | 33% |

Exams
There will be one exam as part of this course. The exam will account for 150 points of your final grade. The exam will be held in classroom unless otherwise specified.

Homework
You will be required to complete three homework assignments during this course. The assignments are focused on cognitive task analysis, GOMS modeling, and use of CogTool or Cogulator to create human performance models.

Article Presentation
During the semester, we will examine a range of research articles. Each student is required to prepare one short presentation based on a designated course reading and deliver them at the beginning of class. The presentation should clearly and concisely summarize the article's main insights for a general audience, using no more than 10 slides and lasting no longer than 10 minutes. Following the presentation, there will be a brief question-and-answer period. Presentations will be evaluated on the quality of the content, the effectiveness of the delivery, and the clarity and accuracy of responses to questions.

Term Project
For your final project, you will be asked to work in teams of 2-3 members to analyze an existing interface using one of the contemporary human performance modeling techniques (i.e., NGOMSL, CPM-GOMS, ACT-R, EPIC, QN-MHP) and using one of the software tools introduced in the course (e.g., Cogulator, CogTool). Your selected interface should be an existing in-vehicle or healthcare-related information technology system. You should provide sufficient motivation for your selection of the interface (e.g., conduct a literature review, frequently used devices that cause errors and/or incidents). Your Project has two Phases and deliverables:
• Phase 1: This phase includes conducting a literature review and selecting the interface that you would like to study. Each team should prepare a PowerPoint presentation (no more than 10 slides) to provide justification for their selection. Each team will have 10 min to present and all team members must participate in the oral presentation.
• Phase 2: Each team should analyze their selected interface using a use case scenarios and human performance modeling. Each team should compare the results of their cognitive models with other empirical usability evaluation techniques (e.g., user testing, survey, heuristic evaluation) for further validation. Each group will provide an oral presentation of the results of their project at the end of the semester. Each team has 20 minutes to present their project. All team members must participate in oral presentation, which will be graded individually. The slides should be submitted prior to the presentation day and via email.

Grades will be determined on the basis of total points earned.
A: 90-100% B: 80-89% C: 70-79% D: 60-69% F: 59% and lower

Grading Policy Changes – Students have 1 week after grades are released for an assignment or exam to submit a re-grade request in writing. This request must not exceed 1 page (11 point font, single spacing), and must clearly indicate the relevant problem(s) and justification for why you think re-grading is warranted. Note that a requested re-grade may result in further point deductions if new errors are discovered.

chunks.title: Graded Attendance
chunks.content:
Students are expected to attend all class lectures except for university-excused absences. It is the student's responsibility to contact the course instructor to make up missed assignments and quizzes within 1 week of returning from the university-excused absence, as well as to submit the required written documentation.
Excused absences: Refer to Texas A&M University policies at http://student-rules.tamu.edu/rule07

Late Work Policy
Generally, homework assignments, article presentations, and projects that are submitted after the due date/time will not receive any credit.
